blocks.text: Unit 4. Dynamic Memory

Pointers Pointers are a fundamental part of the C language, since they provide great flexibility to programs. A pointer is a variable that references a region of memory; in other words, it is a variable whose value is a memory address.
If you have a variable ' p ' of pointer type that contains a memory address where a value ' v ' is stored, it is said that ' p ' points to ' v '.
Pointers are used explicitly with: arrays, structures, and functions. The C/C++ language requires a definition for each variable. To define a pointer variable, the following syntax must be followed:
dataType *variableName; where:

dataType can be any type (int, char, float, struct, etc.), *variableName represents "pointer to", that is, the pointer variable can hold a memory address where a value of the data type specified in the declaration is found.
Examples:

int *ptr;    //pointer to an integer value char *name;  //pointer to a character value float *grade;//pointer to a real (float) value Pointers are dynamic variables that store possible available memory addresses; these addresses are stored in the memory heap, although it is first necessary to reserve enough space for the data type we intend to store.

The following example creates two variables — one integer and one pointer — and we will notice that the pointer stores the address of the integer variable and from that address it manipulates its content. Keep in mind that the pointer never loses its address, but its content (memory address) can be redirected to any memory variable. Due to this characteristic of pointers, their usefulness in handling complex structures is paramount.
int main(){
int A = 7;       // integer variable initialized to 7

int *ptr = NULL; // pointer initialized to 0 cout << "address of A " << &A << " and ptr " << &ptr << endl << "value in A " << A << " and in ptr " << ptr << "\n"; // (b) ptr = &A;        // ptr takes the address of A cout << "address of A " << &A << endl << "address of ptr " << &ptr << " its value points to " << ptr << "\n"; cout << "value in A " << A << " content in *ptr " << *ptr << endl; // (c) *ptr = 14;       // the value of A is now 14 cout << "value in A " << A << " content in *ptr " << *ptr << endl; return 0;

The output generated by this program is as follows (remember, memory addresses may change from machine to machine); notice how the pointer ptr takes the address of A and manipulates its content. In C/C++ the address operator & and indirection operator * are the same:

address of A 0022FF64 and ptr 0022FF68 value in A   7 and in ptr  00000000 address of A   0022FF64 address of ptr 0022FF68 its value points to 0022FF64 value in A  7  and content in *ptr 7 value of A 14  and content in *ptr 14 The images show the phases of the code where the values for each variable are adjusted, as well as the addresses they would occupy in memory. In (a) we have ptr pointing to NULL and variable A with the initialized value; in (b) we see how ptr is assigned the address of A; finally in (c) we see that we can manipulate the content of variable A using the indirection operator through pointer ptr.

It is important to remember that a pointer is a variable and therefore must occupy space in memory; generally, any type of pointer will occupy the same amount of bytes, and its size will depend on the characteristics of the processor and the operating system being used. For our example, we assume the use of an x86 machine with a 32-bit operating system; therefore, all pointers on this platform will occupy at most 4 bytes. Thus, ptr will use the addresses ranging between 0x0022FF68 and 0x0022FF6B; the number of bits used by the aforementioned pointer makes sense, as 4 bytes are necessary to store 32-bit memory addresses Pointer Arithmetic As already seen, pointers are variables that store information — not only about the address they point to — but also about the type of variable stored at that address. This means that operations that make no sense with variable addresses, such as multiplication or division, are not allowed, but others such as addition or subtraction are. Moreover, these operations are performed in a correct but non-ordinary way. Thus, the statement:
causes p to point to the next address from the one it pointed to, taking the data type into account.

For example, if the value pointed to by p is short int and occupies 2 bytes, adding 1 to p means adding 2 bytes to the address it contains, whereas if p points to a double, adding 1 to it means adding 8 bytes.

The difference between pointers of the same type of variable also makes sense. The result is the distance between the addresses of the variables pointed to by them, not in bytes but in data of that same type. The following expressions make full sense in C:

p++;         p--; ++p;         --p; Relationship Between Arrays and Pointers There is a very close relationship between arrays and pointers. The name of an array is a pointer to the memory address that contains the first element of the array.

Note: it is important to remember that the name of an array, despite being a pointer, represents a constant pointer, in the sense that it cannot point to a variable other than the one it already points to.
Suppose the following declarations and statements:
double array[10]; // array is a pointer to array[0]
double *p; ...
p = &array[0];    // p = array;

The identifier array, that is, the name of the array, is a pointer to the first element of the array[]. This is the same as saying that the value of array is &array[0]. There are more points of agreement between arrays and pointers:
Since the name of an array is a pointer, it obeys the laws of pointer arithmetic. Therefore:
if array points to array[0], (array + 1) will point to array[1], and (array + i) will point to array[i].
Conversely, subscripts can be applied to pointers, just as to arrays. So, if p points to array[0], one can write:
p[3] = p[2]*2.0; // equivalent to array[3] = array[2]*2.0;
Assuming p = array, the relationship between pointers and arrays can be summarized as follows:

*p        is equivalent to array[0], to *array and to p[0] *(p + 1)  is equivalent to array[1], to *(array + 1) and to p[1] *(p + i)  is equivalent to array[i], to *(array + i) and to p[i] As an example of the relationship between arrays and pointers, different ways to sum the N elements of an array vec[] are presented. Suppose the following declaration and statements:
int vec[N], sum, i, *p;
for(i=0, sum=0; i < N; ++i)   // form 1
sum += vec[i];
for(i=0, sum=0; i < N; ++i)   // form 2
sum += *(vec+i);
for(p=vec, i=0, sum=0; i < N; ++i)   // form 3
sum += p[i];
for(p=vec, sum=0; p < &vec[N]; ++p)  // form 4
sum += *p;

Pointer to Pointer A pointer stores the memory address where a data value is found; a pointer to a pointer stores the memory address of another pointer. The pointer-to-pointer notation requires a double asterisk, **. Example:

On one hand we have the value stored in variable 'a'; the pointer 'pt1' stores the address of that variable; and the pointer 'pt2' stores the address of the pointer 'pt1'. These are three identifiers, each with a dual aspect: the memory location where it resides, and the value it stores at that memory location.
Declaration and initialization Memory address (hypothetical) Value stored at that memory address
int a = 4;
0xfff6 4
int* pt1 = &a;
0xfff4 0xfff6
int**pt2 = &pt1;
0xfff2 0xfff4 It is interesting to check the different screen outputs of 'pt2' in the following cases:
cout<<pt2;   //Prints the address of pointer 'pt2' itself, here: "0xfff2" cout<<*pt2;  //Prints the address stored in 'pt2', "0xfff4" cout<<**pt2; //Prints the value stored in '*pt1 = a', "4".
The behavior of the screen output is consistent, since the following equalities hold:
*pt2 == pt1;         //Dereferencing 'pt2' *((*pt2)) == *(pt1);    //We apply '*' to both sides *pt1 == a;           //From this and the previous line it follows...
**pt2 == a;           //...this equality The previous examples should be interpreted as "equalities" (comparisons that yield either True or False) and not as assignments.
The close relationship between the concepts of pointer and array is the reason why the double asterisk (**) can be interpreted interchangeably as a pointer to a pointer, or as an array of pointers.
Dynamic Memory

Memory management can be done using static and dynamic data; in the case of static data, their size and form is constant during program execution and is therefore determined at compile time — this is the case for arrays. Static structures have some design limitations, since it is necessary to declare them in advance, the memory space is often not fully occupied, potentially resulting in a waste of memory resources.

On the other hand, dynamic structures often have many benefits; their size is variable throughout a program, so they are created and destroyed at runtime. This allows the data structure to be sized precisely, since memory space is allocated at runtime as needed — this is the case for pointers.

When the operating system loads a program to execute it, it is assigned four logical parts in main memory: text, data, stack, and a free zone (heap). The free zone or memory heap is the area where dynamic space is allocated; due to its nature it tends to be a memory space with high fragmentation because memory links are created and destroyed; it is therefore always recommended to free such spaces, since failing to do so can lead to a saturation of the memory heap. On the other hand, the stack is a memory space that also varies in size, but it is controlled by the operating system.
To work with dynamic data it is necessary to:
Have a programming language that allows us to manage memory dynamically (allocation/deallocation).
Some type of data that allows us to access and manipulate these dynamic data.

Dynamic memory allocation is a programming mechanism that allows us to create variables and arrays dynamically; for this it is necessary to use variables that allow us to access dynamic memory, that is, pointers.

Pointers are dynamic variables that point to possible start addresses of the heap, although it is first necessary to reserve enough space for the data type we intend to store. In that case, the pointer serves as an access key to the beginning of the data block just created in dynamic memory.
Function Description
void* malloc (totalBytes);
Allocates the specified number of bytes.
void* realloc (totalElem, totalBytes);
Increases or decreases the size of the specified memory block. Reallocates if necessary.
void* calloc (totalElem, totalBytes);
Allocates the specified number of bytes and initializes them to zero (0).
void free (void* ptr);
Releases the specified memory block back to the system.

The malloc function, like calloc, returns a void pointer that can be assigned to any type of pointer through a cast. If there was not enough space in the memory heap, both functions return a null (NULL) value indicating that the pointer does not point to any memory address. The calloc function allows generating dynamic arrays of different types, since it has the particularity of initializing all byte locations to zero.

The following example shows how to create a string buffer of a size defined by the user. Remember that there is a close relationship between arrays and pointers in C and C++; the name of an array itself is a pointer to the start of the array, which is why we can manipulate the variable buffer as if it were a character array. On the other hand, we create a dynamic float variable called numero for which we reserve enough memory space to store numbers of that type.
int main() {
char *buffer;
int n = 0;

float *number; cout << "string size? "; cin >> n; cout << "Enter the string "; // it is also possible to use buffer = (char *) calloc(n, sizeof(char)) if ( (buffer = (char *) malloc(n * sizeof(char)) ) == NULL){ cout << "No memory";
else { cin >> buffer; cout << buffer;

free(buffer);
}
cout << "Enter a number ";
number = (float *)calloc(1, sizeof(float));
cin >> *number; cout << *number;
free(number);
return 0;

To store data of different sizes, the sizeof operator is used to determine the number of bytes of a particular data type, including composite types such as structures or unions. For malloc, a simple multiplication is all that is needed to determine the required space.

Once a memory block has been established and used, it will always be necessary to free it to avoid leaving garbage in the memory heap. For this, the free function must be used, which handles this task. The free function only needs to know the start address of the block it will eliminate — that is, the name of the pointer.
In the C++ language, memory management is a somewhat simpler process. There is only one operator that performs memory allocation: the new operator. Its syntax is as follows:
Syntax:
<dataType> * <pointerName>
<pointerName> = new <dataType> <[dimension]>;

The new operator automatically creates enough space in memory to store the indicated type and returns, if applicable, a valid starting memory address. If applied to objects, the constructor of said object will be called. If new is unable to allocate enough space, it returns 0 (NULL). The delete operator will free the memory space requested by the new operator. In the case of objects, space will be freed starting from its destructor. The delete operator only needs the start address of the block — that is, the name of the pointer.
The following example performs exactly the same operations as the previous example, but as a C++ implementation, using the new and delete operators to allocate and free dynamic space.
int main(void) {
char *buffer;
int n = 0;
cout << "string size? "; cin >> n;
cin.ignore();
cout << "enter the string "; if ( (buffer = new char [n]) == NULL ) //allocate array space cout << "No memory"; else { cout << "buffer ";
cin.getline(buffer, n);
cout << "output " << buffer << endl; delete buffer;
cout << "enter a number "; float *number = new float; // allocate space for the float cin >> *number; cout << "value " << *number << endl; delete number;
system("pause");
return 0;

Remember that it is also possible to create more complex structures that allow us to store dynamic data; it is only necessary to remember that accessing dynamic members of a structure type involves the use of other access operators different from the dot (.) that is generally used with simple structures.
The following program involves the construction of dynamic structures, in which we will use a structure with two components and create three different types of variables from them.
twoData simple; cout << "Save values in Simple, integer/real"; cin >> simple.a;      //accesses member a cin >> simple.b;      //accesses member b
cout << "a = " << simple.a << " b = " << simple.b;

twoData *pointer = new twoData; //creates-reserves space for twoData cout << "Save values in Pointer, integer/real "; cin >> pointer->a;       //accesses member a cin >> (*pointer).b;     //accesses member b
cout << "a = "<< (*pointer).a << " b ="<< pointer->b;
delete pointer; cout << "array size ";
int n = 0;
cin >> n; twoData *array = new twoData [n]; //reserve space in memory for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) { cout << "integer real values "; cin >> pointer[i].a;
delete array; return 0;

simple is a data segment variable — that is, it is not a dynamic value; its access to members is through the dot (.) operator. Next we create a dynamic variable of structure type called pointer, note that the access to its members is through the "->" or "(*)." operators, which can be used interchangeably. Finally there is a dynamic array called array of size n entered by the user; note that all access operators for this type of structure are valid, due primarily to the close relationship between pointers and arrays. In reality, in all cases the index serves as a displacement unit between elements (slots).

Arrays as Pointers Due to the close relationship that exists between pointers and arrays, and also because, as we know, an array is essentially a pointer with specific dimensions, we can therefore use a pointer together with the calloc() instruction to design dynamic memory spaces (dynamic arrays).

The calloc() function reserves a specific memory space of a size determined by the first argument and of a data type specified by the second argument. In the following example, a dynamic array of size n is constructed that can hold float data types.
int main(int argc, char *argv[]){
float *array;   // declares a pointer called array
int i, n;
cout << "number of elements "; cin >> n; // reserves n float-type spaces in memory
array = (float *)calloc(n, sizeof(float));
/* if there is not enough memory, returns NULL */ if ( array == NULL){ cout << "no memory"; return 1;
/* we use the pointer array as if it were a vector */ for ( i = 0; i < n; i++ ) { cout << "element " << i; cin >> array [ i ] ;

cout << "the address of the array is " << array; cout << "and the content "; /* we display the content of the dynamic array */ for ( i = 0; i < n; i++ ) cout << array[i]; /* we free the dynamic space */
free( array );
cout << "\n";

Pointers to Functions A function pointer is a variable that stores the memory address of a function. This function can be called later through the pointer. This type of construction is useful because it encapsulates the behavior of a function, which can be called through a pointer.
A pointer can point to code. The declaration looks like this:
void (*ptr_fun) (int, int);
In this case, a variable ptr_fun is being declared that points to a "void" function that accepts two integer parameters.
To take the address of a function, its name is written without parentheses or parameters.
To call a function pointed to by a pointer, the name of the pointer is used as if it were a function.
int sum (int a, int b){
return a + b;

}
int subtract (int a, int b){
return a - b;
// we declare a pointer to functions with two integer // parameters that return an integer

int (*function) (int, int);
{
function = sum;        // 'function' points to 'sum' x = function(4, 3);   // x=sum(4,3) function = subtract;   // 'function' points to 'subtract' x = function(4, 3);   // x=subtract(4,3)
Pointers to functions are also used to implement callback functions.
Callback is a function that is passed to another function as an argument, which is then invoked inside the outer function to complete some kind of routine or action.

Pointers as Parameters In C++ there is parameter passing by value and by reference to a function. In pass-by-value, we know that a local copy is created in the function that stores the same value sent by the argument; furthermore, there is no relationship between those variables so the value of the variable involved in the call is unaffected by changes. Unlike pass-by-value, pass-by-reference allows the invoked function to modify the original value of the variables used as arguments. Later we will see that in C++ it is possible to use two different mechanisms for variable referencing.

In C, pass-by-reference uses the reference operator &, which specifies that the address of the variable will be passed as an argument, such that the function must receive as a parameter a pointer of the same type as the variable — which implies that we can modify the content of that variable, using the pointer as an auxiliary addressing vehicle, aided by the dereference operator (*) on the same pointer.

In C++ the same referencing mechanisms as in C exist, but additionally it is possible to perform a type of reference by creating an alias for the variable identifier; in this way the argument is referenced naturally, and in the function it is received with an alias using the reference operator &, such that it is still the same variable being referenced.
The following example illustrates how parameter passing is performed in C++:
void change( int , int * , int &);
/* The first argument will be passed by value, the next by reference and the last by alias reference */

int main(){
int A = 5, B = 7, C = 10; // A = 5, B = 7, C = 10
cout << "values before the call \n" << "A = "<< A <<" B = "<< B
/* A is passed by value, while B and C go by reference */ change( A, &B, C ); /* after the call to change: A=5, B=9, C=32 */

cout << "values after the call \n" << "A = "<< A <<" B = "<< B << " C = " << C << endl;
}
/* val only copies the value of variable A, while ref receives the address of B, and variable C is received as an alias reference in alias */
void change( int val, int *ref, int &alias ){
val += 7;               // val <- pass-by-value parameter (*ref) += 3;            // ref <- pass-by-reference parameter alias += val + (*ref)--;//alias <- alias reference parameter
values before the call A = 5  B = 7  C = 20 values after the call A = 5  B = 9  C = 32
Addresses and values of the variables in the main() function before the call to change():

Values in the change() function before finishing its execution — that is, the local variables in change(). Variable val receives the value of A (5) and 7 is added; a pointer ref is created pointing to the address of B (7) and indirectly adds 3 and subtracts 1. Finally the variable alias, which is essentially the same variable C — note how it has the same memory address as C, meaning it is the same variable; no local variable was created in the change() function, only the direct reference of the same variable is received.
The values in the main() function after the call to change() — note how A never changed its value, while the other two did.

In the change function, the variable val only copies the value of variable A; this way val is a local variable and when the function ends, that variable will be destroyed. On the other hand, the pointer ref receives the address of variable B, which means its value can be modified using the dereference operator. Finally, variable C is sent to the function and received as an alias reference (alias), which means its address is referenced by alias, so we can modify its content without any problem. The advantage of the "alias or nickname reference" is that we can refer to the same variable by a nickname.

Alias referencing is perhaps one of the programming mechanisms closest to the real concept of reference applied in most object-oriented languages, making it a very useful, simple, and economical referencing mechanism.
